title: Black Coffee on Cotton
timeEstimate: 5–20 minutes (fresh); longer if dried in or hot-set
stainChemistry: Black coffee is a tannin / plant-origin beverage stain — no milk, no cream, no oil. The card is scoped to black coffee only; coffee containing milk or cream is a different stain class and is not handled here. Cotton is cellulose: white cotton is generally tolerant of conservative laundering, while colored cotton requires lower heat and a colorfastness check before any escalation.
whyThisWorks: Cool water rinsing from the back of the fabric carries away most of the fresh coffee. A small amount of liquid laundry detergent lifts remaining residue. Care-label laundering and air-drying with inspection prevent any residual tannin color from being heat-set into the fiber.
defaultAssumption: Most black coffee on cotton is a fresh spill on a washable garment. Fresh black coffee typically responds to conservative cool-water and detergent treatment plus a care-label wash. Coffee that has dried in or already been through a hot wash or dryer is harder and may need a professional dry cleaner.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Confirm fiber, color, and care-label limits. Dry-clean-only or structured pieces escalate to the professional path. Confirm the coffee was black — if it contained milk or cream, route to the combination-stain workflow instead.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Reject too-soiled or non-colorfast garments per care-label guidance. Do not proceed if the care label disallows wet treatment.

spottingProtocol.agent: Cool water rinse from the back
spottingProtocol.instruction: Hold the back of the stained area under cool running water and let the rinse carry the coffee out through the front. Blot underneath with a clean white cotton cloth — do not rub.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Cool water only. Hot water can set the tannin color into the fiber. Avoid mechanical scrubbing.

spottingProtocol.instruction: On colored cotton, dab a small amount of liquid laundry detergent and cool water on an inside seam using a clean white cloth. Press for one minute and check the cloth for color transfer.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Stop if dye transfers. Colored or unstable-dye garments escalate to the professional path.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply a small amount of liquid laundry detergent directly to the stain. Work it in lightly with a fingertip or soft cloth.
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: Brief dwell per product label, then rinse cool
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Follow the detergent product label. Do not mix cleaning agents.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Launder using the temperature setting permitted by the care label; favor cool or lukewarm when uncertain. Rinse thoroughly.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Do not exceed the temperature limit on the care label.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Air-dry the garment and inspect for any residual color before any dryer heat. If a faint shadow remains, repeat the pretreat and laundering step; do not machine-dry until the stain is fully gone.
spottingProtocol.safetyNote: Heat applied to residual tannin can set the color into the fiber.

steps: 1. Identify fiber, dye stability, and care-label limits before any wet treatment; confirm the coffee is black (no milk / cream).
2. Rinse fresh coffee with cool water from the back of the fabric.
3. Apply surfactant pretreat appropriate to the tannin family; rinse and assess.
4. Launder per care-label temperature limit; do not exceed.

5. Air-dry and inspect before any heat. If escalation is warranted, apply a pro tannin product or controlled oxidizer only when the care label permits, colorfastness is confirmed, and PPE / ventilation per product SDS are in place; tannin formulas can shift dye color and must be flushed thoroughly.
products: Cool / lukewarm water per care label
professionalProtocol.temperature: Cool through spotting; care-label cool / lukewarm wash; no heat on residual color
warnings: Do not exceed care-label temperature limits
Hot water on residual tannin can set the color
Confirm colorfastness via hidden-area test before any escalation
Tannin formulas can shift dye color and must be flushed thoroughly
Pro chemistry beyond consumer detergent requires SDS-driven PPE and ventilation

homeSolutions.agent: Black Coffee Only — Confirm and Check Care Label
homeSolutions.instruction: This page is for black coffee — no milk and no cream. If your coffee had milk or cream in it, the chemistry is different; treat it as a combination stain and take it to a professional dry cleaner. Check the care label. If it says dry-clean-only, or the garment is structured / lined / embellished / vintage, stop and take it to a professional dry cleaner. For washable cotton, continue.

homeSolutions.agent: Cool Water Rinse from the Back
homeSolutions.instruction: Flip the fabric inside out and hold the back of the stained area under cool running water for 30–60 seconds. Most fresh black coffee rinses out at this step. Blot underneath with a clean white cloth — do not rub.

On colored cotton, dab a little liquid laundry detergent and cool water on an inside seam with a clean white cloth. Press one minute. If color transfers to the cloth, stop and take to a professional. White cotton can skip this step.
Put a small amount of mild liquid laundry detergent directly on what remains of the stain. Work it in gently with your fingertip or a soft cloth — do not scrub.
Wash the garment using the temperature your care label allows. If you are unsure, use cool or lukewarm water. Rinse thoroughly.

homeSolutions.agent: Air-Dry and Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Air-dry the garment and look closely for any remaining shadow before putting it in the dryer. If a faint mark remains, repeat steps 4 and 5 once. Do not machine-dry until the stain is fully gone — heat can set residual tannin into cotton.

If a shadow is still there after two careful passes, or if the stain has already been through a hot wash or dryer, take the garment to a professional dry cleaner. Tell them: "Black coffee on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with cool rinse, liquid laundry detergent, and a care-label wash."

steps: 1. Confirm coffee was BLACK (no milk / cream). Read care label; escalate dry-clean-only / structured / vintage to a pro.
2. Cool water rinse from the BACK of the fabric.
3. On colored cotton, hidden-area colorfastness test before detergent.
4. Apply a small amount of liquid laundry detergent.
5. Launder per care label (favor cool / lukewarm if uncertain).
6. Air-dry and inspect for any residual shadow before any dryer heat.
7. Repeat once if needed; escalate if it doesn't clear or was heat-set.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the coffee contained milk or cream (different stain class), the care label is dry-clean-only, the garment is structured / lined / embellished, the hidden-area test shows color transfer, the stain has already been through a hot wash or dryer, or two conservative passes do not clear it. Escalate to a professional dry cleaner in any of those cases.
warnings: Black coffee only — coffee with milk or cream is a different stain class
Do not use hot water on aged or dried coffee — can set the tannin
Do not rub or scrub — spreads the stain
Do not machine-dry residual color — heat can set the stain
Pro-strength oxidizers, solvents, and tannin acids are not Home steps

neverDo: Never machine-dry a garment with residual coffee color — heat can set the tannin
Never treat coffee containing milk or cream with this protocol — it is a combination stain
Never rub or scrub the stain aggressively
Never use chlorine bleach on colored cotton
Never apply professional-strength oxidizers, solvents, or tannin acids in the Home path
fiberSensitivities: Cotton is cellulose; white cotton is generally tolerant of conservative laundering
Colored cotton requires lower heat and a colorfastness check before escalation
Aged or hot-dried tannin residue is harder to remove and may need a professional dry cleaner
chemicalBoundaries: Home path: mild liquid laundry detergent and water only
No peroxide, oxygen bleach, or chlorine bleach as a default Home step
Pro path may use controlled tannin chemistry or oxidizers only when care label permits, colorfastness is confirmed, and SDS-driven PPE and ventilation are in place
homeAllowed: Cool or lukewarm water per care label
Clean white cotton cloth for blotting
Air-drying and visual inspection before any heat
proOnly: Pro tannin formulas, concentrated surfactants, and oxidizers used in a controlled professional environment with PPE / ventilation per product SDS; tannin chemistry can shift dye color and must be flushed thoroughly
testFirst: Hidden-area colorfastness test (white cloth on an inside seam) before detergent dwell or any escalation on colored cotton
flushNeutralizeDry: Rinse thoroughly with cool water from the back of the fabric
Rinse again after each detergent application
Air-dry and inspect before any dryer heat
escalationTriggers: Coffee contained milk or cream (combination stain)
Care label is dry-clean-only or restricts wet treatment
Garment is structured / lined / embellished / vintage / high-value
Stain has already been through a hot wash or dryer
Two conservative passes do not clear the stain
ppeVentilation: Follow the product label for any concentrate
Gloves and eye protection for any pro-strength chemistry
Ventilate the work area; keep cleaning agents away from children and pets
safetyMatrix.homeVsProBoundary: Home users use only cool-water rinsing and mild liquid laundry detergent with a care-label wash. Pro chemistry (concentrated surfactants, tannin formulas, oxidizers) is hidden from the Home path and requires SDS-driven PPE / ventilation in a controlled environment.

materialWarnings: Black coffee only — coffee with milk or cream is a different stain class
Cool water only — hot water can set the tannin
Inspect for any residual shadow before any dryer heat
Aged or hot-dried coffee is harder to remove and may need a pro

escalation.when: Coffee contained milk or cream; dry-clean-only, structured, or vintage cotton; colored cotton that fails a hidden-area test; coffee that has been through a hot wash or dryer; or no improvement after two conservative passes.
escalation.whatToTell: "Black coffee on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with cool rinse, liquid laundry detergent, and a care-label wash. [Hot wash or dryer applied? — yes / no]."

contextQuestions: Was the coffee actually black (no milk / cream)?
How old is the stain — fresh, overnight, or older?
White, light, or dark / dyed cotton?
Has the garment been through a hot wash or dryer since the spill?
Is the garment dry-clean-only, structured, or embellished?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Black coffee is a single-component tannin stain and is the simpler of the coffee variants. Adding milk or cream changes the chemistry and is out of scope for this card — escalate or use the combination-stain workflow instead. Heat-set or aged tannin residue is meaningfully harder and should be escalated to a professional dry cleaner.

sources.title: University Stain Science (compiled extension reference)
sources.evidence: Coffee fits the tannin family; conservative unknown-stain fallback (cool water, liquid detergent, rinse, air-dry); blot rather than rub; do not iron or dry stained fabric until removal is confirmed.

sources.title: EPA Alternative Cleaning Curriculum
sources.evidence: Surfactant action of detergents; test new detergents on inconspicuous areas; follow product labels and PPE / ventilation for concentrates; clean per care label or reject inappropriate garments.

sources.title: GONR Material Safety Matrix v2
sources.evidence: Cotton fiber tolerances: white cotton tolerant, colored cotton requires lower heat and no chlorine bleach or strong peroxide; tannin / coffee escalation chemistry restricted to white / colorfast fabric with control; tannin removers are pro-context; absolute safety gates including no cleaner-mixing.

sources.title: American Drycleaner Spotting Techniques
sources.evidence: Coffee is plant-origin / tannin group; tannin formulas are acidic, need full flushing, and can shift dye color.
